citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
The licensee is not following their plan for bird environment enhancement. Their plan states that birds with feather
plucking, a sign of psychological distress, will be isolated from the flock. One white female chicken that has a moderate
amount of feather loss along her left wing and back has not been isolated from the flock. The licensee stated that they
have noticed that she tends to pluck her own feathers.
It is important to give special attention to birds that show any signs of psychological distress to provide adequate care,
alleviate any suffering, and promote welfare. Not following an environment enhancement plan for special attention for
birds with psychological distress can lead to prolonged distress for the animal. Dealers and exhibitors must develop,
document, and follow a species-appropriate plan for environment enhancement adequate to promote the psychological
well-being of birds including special attention for birds showing signs of psychological distress through behavior or
appearance.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
A dog kennel that is being used inside the chicken enclosure has a buildup of excreta. The chickens use this kennel as
shelter and for perching. Located above the kennel is the accumulation of old and new bird excreta. Bird excreta has
fallen onto the kennels left side as indicated by dark brown layers of dirt and old excreta along the wall. This excrement is
dark in color and has formed a round mass above the projecting rim.
An accumulation of feces can increase the risk of disease, pests, and odors. The facility must remove excreta and food
wase from the primary enclosure as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulation of feces and food waste, to
prevent soiling of the birds contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by 06/05/2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

narrative:
3.154(c)(2) 
   
Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being. 
The licensee is not following their plan for bird environment enhancement. Their plan states that birds with feather 
plucking, a sign of psychological distress, will be isolated from the flock. One white female chicken that has a moderate 
amount of feather loss along her left wing and back has not been isolated from the flock. The licensee stated that they 
have noticed that she tends to pluck her own feathers. 
 
It is important to give special attention to birds that show any signs of psychological distress to provide adequate care, 
alleviate any suffering, and promote welfare. Not following an environment enhancement plan for special attention for 
birds with psychological distress can lead to prolonged distress for the animal. Dealers and exhibitors must develop, 
document, and follow a species-appropriate plan for environment enhancement adequate to promote the psychological 
well-being of birds including special attention for birds showing signs of psychological distress through behavior or 
appearance. 
3.158(a)(1) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
A dog kennel that is being used inside the chicken enclosure has a buildup of excreta. The chickens use this kennel as 
shelter and for perching. Located above the kennel is the accumulation of old and new bird excreta. Bird excreta has 
fallen onto the kennels left side as indicated by dark brown layers of dirt and old excreta along the wall. This excrement is 
dark in color and has formed a round mass above the projecting rim. 
 
An accumulation of feces can increase the risk of disease, pests, and odors. The facility must remove excreta and food 
wase from the primary enclosure as often as necessary to prevent excessive accumulation of feces and food waste, to 
prevent soiling of the birds contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and odors. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant.

All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by 06/05/2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
